hub_subtitle: Conversação para viagens, listening de verdade e reuniões internacionais — rumo a Nova York, setembro de 2026

lesson.menu_desc: A New York restaurant with a business guest: read the daily specials, ask about allergens, send back a dish, and split the check -- all in natural American English. The words of the table (server, daily special, house specialty, to eat out, to go with, to split the check, leftovers, on the house, allergen, dressing on the side, well-done, happy hour) and the grammar that lets you imagine any meal: the second conditional -- 'If I were you, I would go with the house specialty', 'If the server recommended it, I would try it' -- contrasted with the first conditional. -- 36 slides
lesson.subtitle: Lesson 18 -- Ordering with Confidence
lesson.title_tag: Professor View -- Lucimara | Lesson 18 -- Ordering with Confidence

listenings.text: Good evening, and welcome. Let me tell you about our specials tonight. Our daily special is a pan-seared salmon with a lemon butter sauce, served with roasted vegetables and a small salad. The house specialty, the one people come back for, is our slow-cooked short rib. It is on the menu every day, and honestly, it is what I would order myself. If you have any food allergies, just let me know and the kitchen will take care of it. Oh, and happy hour runs until seven, so your first drink is on the house. Take your time, and I will be right back to take your order.

Here is a simple way to talk about imaginary situations at a restaurant. We call it the second conditional. You take if plus a past-tense verb, and then would plus the base verb. For example, if you were not sure what to order, you would ask the server. Notice that we use were for everybody: if I were you, if she were here. This is different from the first conditional, which is for real, likely things: if they have the salmon, I will order it. Past plus would means it is imaginary. Present plus will means it is real. Get that difference, and you can give advice about any meal.

vocab.title: Match each word to its meaning
defs: a
the person who takes your order and brings your food
b
a dish the restaurant offers only that day, not on the regular menu
c
the dish a restaurant is most famous for
d
to have a meal in a restaurant instead of at home
e
to choose something from the menu
f
to divide the bill so each person pays a share
g
the food you do not finish and take home to eat later
h
free, paid for by the restaurant
i
a food that can cause an allergic reaction, like nuts or shellfish
j
salad sauce served separately so you add your own amount
k
cooked all the way through, with no pink inside
l
a time when drinks and snacks cost less

paras: Dining out in the United States has its own set of unwritten rules. When you sit down, a server greets you and often describes the daily specials, dishes that are not on the regular menu. If you were not sure what to order, a good server would happily recommend the house specialty. Portions are usually large, so no one is surprised if you ask to take your leftovers home.

Money works a little differently too. In most American restaurants, a tip of around twenty percent is expected, because servers depend on it. When friends eat out together, they often split the check evenly instead of arguing over who had what. If a restaurant made a small mistake, the manager might bring you a dessert on the house to keep you happy.

Food preferences are taken seriously. If you told your server that you had a food allergy, the kitchen would treat that allergen with real care. You can ask for your steak well-done, your dressing on the side, or a dish without a certain ingredient, and no one will think it is strange. In the United States, ordering exactly what you want is not rude. It is simply how dining out works.
reading.source: Adapted from a United States dining and culture guide

reading.prompt: What is the main idea of the text?
choices: American restaurants are more expensive than restaurants in other countries.
Dining out in the US follows its own rules for specials, tipping and splitting the check, and asking for exactly what you want is completely normal.
You should never take your leftovers home in the United States.

items: In the US, a server often describes dishes that are not on the regular menu.
When you sit down, a server describes the daily specials, dishes that are not on the regular menu.
Americans think it is rude to take your leftovers home.
Portions are usually large, so no one is surprised if you ask to take your leftovers home.
A tip of around twenty percent is expected in most American restaurants.
In most American restaurants, a tip of around twenty percent is expected, because servers depend on it.
In the US, asking for your dressing on the side is considered strange.
You can ask for your dressing on the side, and no one will think it is strange.

gapfill.text: Second conditional for imaginary situations: if + past simple, would + base verb. 'If I were you, I would order the house specialty.' Use 'were' for all subjects (if I were, if she were). Compare the first conditional (real, likely): 'If they have it, I will order it.'

bank.label: Useful language at the table
items: If I were you, I would go with the house specialty.
If they had a vegetarian option, I would order it.
Could I have the dressing on the side, please?
We would like to split the check, please.
If the steak were too rare, I would send it back.

lf.title: Complete the second conditional
items: rich, I would eat out every night.
If we had more time, we
you, I would ask about the allergens.

lf.text: Second conditional = imaginary. If + past simple, would + base verb. Use 'were' for every subject (if I were, if she were). Keep 'would' short: I'd, she'd, we'd.

items.situation: A friend cannot decide what to eat. Give advice starting with 'If I were you...'.
tips: Use 'If I were you, I would...'.
'If I were you, I would go with the house specialty.'

items.situation: Imagine you had no dietary limits at all. What would you order?
tips: 'If I had no limits, I would order the ribeye.'

items.situation: Your steak arrives too rare. What would you do?
tips: 'If my steak were too rare, I would send it back.'
Stay polite: 'I would ask them to cook it a bit more.'

items.situation: You won a free dinner anywhere in New York. Where would you go and what would you order?
tips: 'If I won a free dinner, I would go to...'.
Add a second clause: '...and I would order...'.
would + base verb after the if-clause.

items.situation: Your business guest has a nut allergy. What would you tell the server?
tips: 'If my guest ate nuts, it would be dangerous, so...'.
'I would tell the server about the allergen.'
allergen = a food that can cause a reaction.

items: The server asks what you would recommend to a first-time visitor. Answer with 'If I were them, I would...' and the house specialty.
A friend is nervous about ordering in English. Give two pieces of advice using 'If I were you, I would...'.
Imagine your dream meal in New York. Say where you would go, what you would order, and who you would bring, using the second conditional.
Your guest cannot eat gluten. Explain what you would ask the server, using 'If... I would...' at least twice.

answerkey.title: Reveal the answer key
list: Gap-fill: 1 = server, 2 = specials, 3 = leftovers, 4 = split, 5 = on the house.
Second conditional: if + past simple, would + base verb. 'If I were you, I would order the special.'
Use 'were' for all subjects: if I were, if he were, if she were.
First vs second: 'If they have it, I will order it' (real) vs 'If they had it, I would order it' (imaginary).
answerkey.note: Second conditional = imaginary. If + past, would + base. 'were' for everybody.
